name: Uchaihshravas
description: Uchaihshravas is a seven-headed divine horse that emerged during the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana) in Hindu mythology. It is considered a celestial steed, a symbol of power, speed, and purity. According to some accounts, Uchaihshravas was initially white, but its color changed to black due to a curse placed upon it by Kadru, the mother of the Nagas. This curse stemmed from a dispute between Kadru and Vinata, the mother of Garuda, regarding the true color of the horse's tail. The magnificent horse is often associated with Indra, the king of the gods, who is sometimes depicted as its rider. Uchaihshravas represents ultimate strength and is revered for its divine origins. The story of Uchaihshravas also highlights themes of truth, deception, and the consequences of dishonesty. The horse's seven heads signify its immense power and ability to perceive the world from multiple perspectives simultaneously. Its celestial nature connects it to the heavens and the divine realm, making it a highly respected figure in Hindu lore. It appears in various Hindu scriptures and is a significant symbol in art and literature, representing royalty and the power of the gods.
subtitle: Seven-headed divine horse of the gods

keyMyths.title: The Churning of the Ocean
keyMyths.description: Uchaihshravas emerged during the Samudra Manthana when the gods and demons churned the ocean to obtain amrita (the nectar of immortality). This event highlights the horse's divine origin and its connection to the cosmos.

keyMyths.title: The Curse of Kadru
keyMyths.description: Kadru cursed Uchaihshravas to have a black tail due to a disagreement with Vinata. This curse highlights themes of deception and the consequences of dishonesty in mythology.

sources.author: Vyasa
sources.description: The epic Hindu text containing the story of Uchaihshravas's birth and the curse placed upon it.
sources.text: The epic Hindu text containing the story of Uchaihshravas's birth and the curse placed upon it.
